summary.scholar: On October 29, 1929 — 'Black Tuesday' — the stock market crashed catastrophically, losing $14 billion in value in a single day. The crash triggered the Great Depression, the worst economic crisis in American history. Banks failed, businesses closed, and unemployment eventually reached 25%. The depression lasted throughout the 1930s and only fully ended with the industrial mobilization for World War II.
summary.explorer: On October 29, 1929 — called 'Black Tuesday' — the stock market crashed and America's economy fell apart. Banks closed, businesses shut down, and one out of every four people lost their job. The Great Depression lasted for years and was the worst economic disaster in American history.

significance.scholar: The crash of 1929 exposed the fragility of unregulated financial markets and led to fundamental reforms including the creation of the SEC, FDIC, and Social Security.
significance.explorer: This crash changed how America handles money forever. The government created new rules and programs to try to prevent it from happening again.

sources.citation: Miller Center: U.S. Presidents and Key Events
